name: Palaces and Casonas of Camargo Route
name_original: Ruta circular por las Casonas y Palacios de Camargo
description: Explore Camargo's historic casonas and palacios on this 11.1-mile (17.9 km) touring cycling route with moderate difficulty.
region_intro: Touring cycling routes around Camargo, located in Cantabria, Spain, traverse a diverse landscape characterized by a coastal strip with low, wide, and gently rolling valleys. The region features river estuaries, beaches, and transitions into more mountainous terrain to the south. These physical features offer varied elevation profiles for touring cyclists. Best touring cycling routes around Camargo The most popular touring cycling route is Palaces and Casonas of Camargo Route, a 11.1 miles (17.9 km) trail that takes 1 hour 8 minutes to complete. This moderate route explores cultural landmarks and historical buildings within the Camargo area. Another top favourite among local touring cyclists is Puente Viesgo – Reyerma Steam Locomotive loop from Maliaño - La Vidriera, a moderate 31.1 miles (50.1 km) path. This route offers a scenic loop, potentially following an old railway line through varied terrain. Local touring cyclists also love the Playa de la Magdalena – Centro Botín loop from Muriedas-Bahía, a 13.1 miles (21.0 km) trail leading through coastal areas and urban points of interest, often completed in about 1 hour 18 minutes. Touring cycling around Camargo is defined by coastal paths, rolling valleys, and river estuaries. The network offers options for different ability levels, from easy coastal rides to more challenging routes with greater elevation. The routes in Camargo are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.4 stars from over 27 reviews. More than 380 touring cyclists have used komoot to explore Camargo's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many touring cycling routes are available in Camargo?
A: There are over 20 touring cycling routes around Camargo, offering a diverse range of experiences for different skill levels. The komoot community has rated these routes highly, with an average score of 4.4 stars from over 27 reviews.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on touring cycling routes in Camargo?
A: Touring cycling routes in Camargo traverse a varied landscape. You'll find a coastal strip with low, wide, and gently rolling valleys, river estuaries, and beaches. As you head south, the terrain transitions into more mountainous areas, offering routes with greater elevation changes. The region's Atlantic climate means you can expect a humid oceanic environment with mild winters and warm summers.

Q: What is the best time of year for touring cycling in Camargo?
A: Camargo experiences a humid oceanic climate with warm summers and mild winters, influenced by Atlantic Ocean winds. This generally makes it suitable for touring cycling for much of the year. Summer offers warmer temperatures, while spring and autumn provide pleasant conditions with potentially fewer crowds. Always check local weather forecasts before heading out.

Q: Are there touring cycling routes suitable for beginners in Camargo?
A: Yes, Camargo offers several easy touring cycling routes perfect for beginners. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38204330" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Playa de la Magdalena – Centro Botín loop from Muriedas-Bahía&lt;/a&gt; is an easy 13.1-mile (21.0 km) trail that leads through coastal areas and urban points of interest, typically completed in about 1 hour 18 minutes.

Q: Are there challenging touring cycling routes in Camargo for experienced riders?
A: Yes, for experienced touring cyclists seeking a challenge, Camargo has more difficult routes. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/40311283" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puerto de La Braguía – Pas Greenway (Astillero) loop from Maliaño - La Vidriera&lt;/a&gt; is a difficult 49.4-mile (79.4 km) route with significant elevation gain, providing a demanding ride through varied terrain.

Q: What natural features or landmarks can I see along the touring cycling routes in Camargo?
A: Along the touring cycling routes in Camargo, you can explore a variety of natural features. Highlights include coastal areas like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3635532" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Somocuevas Beach&lt;/a&gt;, unique geological formations such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4992502" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Liencres Blowhole&lt;/a&gt;, and serene bodies of water like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3984180" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Pozo Menor de Valcaba&lt;/a&gt; and &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/6037295" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;PozoMayor&lt;/a&gt;. The region also features river estuaries and diverse plant life zones.

Q: Are there any cultural or historical points of interest to explore while cycling in Camargo?
A: Absolutely. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1012454904/palaces-and-casonas-of-camargo-route" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Palaces and Casonas of Camargo Route&lt;/a&gt; is specifically designed to explore cultural landmarks and historical buildings within the Camargo area. You can also find interesting sites like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3758478" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cave of the Virgen del Mar&lt;/a&gt;, offering a glimpse into the region's heritage.

Q: Are there family-friendly touring cycling routes in Camargo?
A: Many of the easier routes in Camargo, particularly those along the coastal strip with low and gently rolling valleys, are suitable for families. These routes often feature less elevation and provide a pleasant experience for riders of all ages. Always check the route's difficulty and length to ensure it matches your family's capabilities.

Q: Can I bring my dog on touring cycling routes in Camargo?
A: While specific regulations for dogs on cycling routes can vary, generally, dogs are welcome on many outdoor trails in Spain, provided they are kept on a leash and you clean up after them. It's always best to check local signage or specific park rules for any restrictions, especially in protected natural areas. Consider your dog's fitness level for longer rides.

Q: Are there circular touring cycling routes in Camargo?
A: Yes, many touring cycling routes in Camargo are designed as loops, allowing you to start and end at the same point. Examples include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/40640630" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puente Viesgo – Reyerma Steam Locomotive loop from Maliaño - La Vidriera&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38204330" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Playa de la Magdalena – Centro Botín loop from Muriedas-Bahía&lt;/a&gt;, which are popular choices among local cyclists.

Q: What do other touring cyclists enjoy the most about touring cycling in Camargo?
A: The komoot community highly rates touring cycling in Camargo, with an average score of 4.4 stars. Reviewers often praise the diverse terrain, from coastal paths to rolling valleys, and the opportunity to explore both natural beauty and cultural landmarks. The network of routes offers options for different ability levels, making it enjoyable for many.

Q: Are there options for public transport to access touring cycling routes in Camargo?
A: Camargo is part of Cantabria, a region with public transport options. Depending on your starting point, you may be able to use local buses or trains to get closer to trailheads. It's advisable to check the specific public transport routes and their bike carriage policies in advance, as these can vary.

Q: Where can I find parking near touring cycling trailheads in Camargo?
A: Parking availability will vary depending on the specific trailhead. In more urban or popular coastal areas, you might find designated parking lots. For routes starting in smaller towns or natural areas, street parking might be an option. It's recommended to check the starting point of your chosen route on a map for potential parking facilities.
tips: It is a quadrangular building with a four-sided roof, with the brick wall of the main façade placed horizontally on edge and arranged in an orthogonal wooden grid and masonry and ashlar gables in the openings and corners. The farm is accessed through an 18th century doorway whose frieze contains a very deteriorated shield in which Velarde's arms could be seen. The building was built at the end of the 17th century, beginning of the 18th century. At the end of the 18th century, the army captain Pedro Velarde y Santiyán lived in this mansion. He was the protagonist, together with Captain Luis Daoiz, of the uprising that took place on May 2, 1808 in Madrid against the Napoleonic troops and cost both soldiers their lives. The house and the farm were declared in 1985 Asset of Cultural Interest with the category of Historic Site and since 1966 continents of the Ethnographic Museum of Cantabria.

The Igollo Architectural Complex is made up of a group of beautiful old houses located on the road towards Escobedo, such as the Herrera tower house in Igollo, commissioned by the Knight of Alcántara Tormas de Herrera, General Treasurer of the Holy Crusade and family of the Inquisition in 1680; The mansion of Benito de la Torre Herrera (Igollo), which is estimated to have been built at the behest of Tomás de Herrera at the end of the 17th century; and The doorway of the house of the Puente Herrera family (Igollo), from the second half of the 17th century.

The Palace of the Marqués de Villapuente, located in Muriedas and the seat of the Camargo City Council, is one of the most important civil baroque buildings in Cantabria. It was built around 1727 and is made up of two floors to which is attached a beautiful three-story fortress tower built in ashlar masonry from the Escobedo quarries. It has three shields, among which the City Hall and the coat of arms of the Marquis de Villapuente, former owner of the palace, stand out.

faqs.question: How long does it typically take to complete this cycling route?
faqs.answer: The route is approximately 17.9 kilometers long and takes about 1 hour and 7 minutes to complete by touring bicycle, depending on your pace and how often you stop to explore the historical sites.

faqs.question: What kind of terrain can I expect on the route?
faqs.answer: The route primarily features paved surfaces, making it suitable for touring bicycles and a wide range of cyclists. While generally smooth, some gradual inclines and descents are present, consistent with its moderate difficulty rating.

faqs.question: What historical landmarks will I see along the way?
faqs.answer: This route is designed to showcase the rich architectural heritage of Camargo. You'll encounter several notable historical sites, including the Casa de Velarde and Ethnographic Museum of Cantabria, the Igollo Architectural Ensemble, and the impressive Palace of the Marquis of Villapuente, which now serves as the Camargo Town Hall.

faqs.question: Is this cycling route suitable for families or beginners?
faqs.answer: Yes, despite its 'moderate' classification, the route's predominantly paved surfaces and manageable elevation gain of 190 meters make it accessible for most cyclists, including families with children and those with good fitness levels who are new to longer routes. It's a great way to combine gentle exercise with cultural exploration.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: Cantabria's climate is generally mild. Spring (April-June) and early autumn (September-October) are often ideal, offering pleasant temperatures and lush scenery. Summer can also be enjoyable, though it might be warmer. Always check the local weather forecast before you go.

faqs.question: Where can I park to access the route?
faqs.answer: While specific parking areas for this exact route aren't detailed, Camargo has various public parking options, especially near its main towns like Muriedas where some of the palaces are located. It's advisable to look for parking in central Camargo areas that offer easy access to the start of the circular route.

faqs.question: Are dogs allowed on this cycling route?
faqs.answer: Generally, cycling routes on paved surfaces in urban or semi-urban areas like this one are dog-friendly, provided dogs are kept on a leash and waste is properly disposed of. However, always be mindful of local regulations and other cyclists. Some historical sites might have restrictions for pets inside buildings.

faqs.question: Do I need any special permits or pay an entrance fee to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: No, you do not need any special permits or to pay an entrance fee to cycle this public route through Camargo. Access to the historical sites themselves may have separate entry fees if you wish to go inside.

faqs.question: Are there any cafes, restaurants, or accommodation options nearby?
faqs.answer: Yes, as the route passes through Camargo, you'll find various cafes, restaurants, and accommodation options in the towns and villages along the way, particularly in Muriedas. These provide convenient spots for refreshments or an overnight stay.

faqs.question: What should I bring for this cycling tour?
faqs.answer: For this moderate cycling tour, we recommend bringing a touring bicycle in good condition, a helmet, water, snacks, sun protection, and a basic repair kit. A camera is also a great idea to capture the beautiful architecture and landscapes.

faqs.question: Is public transport available to reach the starting point of the route?
faqs.answer: Camargo is well-connected by public transport within Cantabria. You can likely reach the area by bus or train, depending on your starting location. Check local transport schedules for routes to Camargo, particularly to areas like Muriedas, which are central to the historical sites on the tour.
